ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fish cake entry; no specific breaded or deep-fried fish cake fillet term exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers breaded/battered fish products; no exact code for Singaporean-style processed fish paste cake fillet. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for breaded fish cake fillet; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one breaded fish cake fillet piece weighing approximately 60 g, commonly consumed as a snack or breakfast item in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Breaded fish cake fillet — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Breaded fish cake fillet (cr_id F03040001). All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient fields not reported by HPB for this entry; set to -1 where unavailable.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Breaded/Battered Fish Products — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for breaded fish products and refined wheat breadcrumb-coated foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific Singaporean breaded fish cake fillet in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 75 (high) is estimated by analogy to breaded and battered fish products with refined wheat breadcrumb coatings, which are documented in the University of Sydney GI Database as high-GI foods (GI range 70–80). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 20.8 × 0.60) / 100 = (75 × 12.48) / 100 = 9.36, rounded to 9.4. The high sugar content (19.3 g/100 g) and refined breadcrumb coating support the high-GI classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 1051 mg per 100 g, far exceeding the 600 mg/100 g threshold for high-sodium foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit consumption of this product; a single 60 g serving delivers approximately 631 mg sodium, representing over 27% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit, and additional sodium from condiments such as chilli or soy sauce will further elevate cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar content (19.3 g/100 g) combined with a high glycemic index (estimated 75) and low dietary fibre (1.5 g/100 g), producing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this food; the combination of high sugar, high-GI refined breadcrumb coating, and minimal fibre (1.5 g/100 g) is expected to cause a sharp postprandial glucose spike, and the high sodium content additionally increases cardiovascular risk which is already elevated in this population.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Critically high sodium (1051 mg/100 g), ultra-processed deep-fried preparation generating advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), and high sugar content collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid this product; the extremely high sodium load promotes hypertension and fluid retention, the deep-fried ultra-processed preparation introduces pro-inflammatory AGEs and likely trans fats, and the high sugar content contributes to dyslipidaemia and endothelial dysfunction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium worsens RA-related tissue oedema and deep-fried preparation generates pro-inflammatory AGEs; no omega-3 or anti-inflammatory benefit despite fish base
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this food; the high sodium (1051 mg/100 g) exacerbates joint swelling, and the deep-frying process generates AGEs that amplify systemic inflammation, while the heavy processing eliminates any potential omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefit from the fish base.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Breaded Fish Cake Fillet is a deep-fried, ultra-processed Singaporean snack food consisting of a rectangular patty made from processed fish paste that is coated with bread crumbs and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The product is commonly sold at hawker centres, food courts, and convenience stores across Singapore, and is typically consumed as a breakfast item or snack, often accompanied by chilli sauce or ketchup. The texture is firm and slightly chewy on the inside from the fish paste, with a crispy, crunchy exterior from the breadcrumb coating. The flavour is mildly savoury with a subtle fish taste, though the heavy processing and seasoning dominate the natural fish flavour. Nutritionally, per 100 g the product provides 162 kcal, 10.1 g protein, 4.3 g fat, 20.8 g carbohydrate, 19.3 g sugar, 1.5 g dietary fibre, and a critically high 1051 mg sodium. The very high sodium content (1051 mg/100 g) makes this product strongly unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The high sugar content (19.3 g/100 g) combined with a high estimated glycemic index of 75 and low fibre makes this food inappropriate for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that contribute to systemic inflammation. Despite being fish-based, the heavy processing eliminates meaningful omega-3 fatty acid benefit. This food should be consumed only occasionally and in small portions by healthy individuals, and avoided by those with chronic metabolic or inflammatory conditions.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean foods. The GI value is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is clearly documented as such. Multilingual names are provided for all four mandatory Singaporean languages (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) in addition to English.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB for this entry): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific processed fish cake fillet product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03040001) as of the access date. The USDA FoodData Central was consulted for supplementary micronutrient data but no sufficiently close match for this specific Singaporean processed fish cake fillet formulation was identified to justify substitution without risk of misrepresentation. Input dietary_tag 'wholegrain' has been removed as this product uses refined breadcrumbs and does not qualify as a wholegrain food. GI value of 75 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement for this product is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated at 60 g serving size: (75 × 12.48) / 100 = 9.4. The input sugar value of 19.3 g/100 g is unusually high for a fish cake product and may reflect added sugars in the seasoning or a data anomaly; this value is preserved as reported by HPB at confidence 0.95 but flagged for follow-up verification. No zero-value core macros are present in this record; the zero-macro justification rule (g4) does not apply.
